allusions.translator_note: The standing pair of monsters of the Strait, taken from Homer; by Cicero's day a commonplace.
The Polyphemus-figure, set in the same Sicilian setting as Homer; Cicero plays with the geographical overlap ("in those same places").
Not an allusion but a quoted Roman citizen's standing self-identification, made famous by this passage and re-used in NT and later. Cicero treats it almost as a religious phrase.

allusions.suspected_source: Homeric / Hellenistic poet-tradition of Sicilian shore-monsters
allusions.translator_note: Cicero refers to "poets" generally for the figure of the shore-monster who waylays ships; the type is Homeric, refracted through Hellenistic poets.
